transcript: It is very possible that humpback whales, which are known to be a social animal, living group, hunt together, have the capacity to cooperate, probably to communicate. It remains speculative, but it is very possible that it is an indication that whales indeed have capacity for high cognitive function. Some of them may be comparable to what we have in humans. When you look at a humpback whale and what a humpback whale can do in water, it's pretty remarkable. Humpback whales are able to function as groups. It involves cognition, memory, learning, and adaptability to a situation. So all this can be construed as a form of intelligence. At the feeding grounds, humpbacks demonstrate a high level of cooperation, such as the strategy they use to maximize fish catches. It's called bubble netting. Up to 20 of them work together to harvest a swirling ball of fish. One whale encircles them in a ring of bubbles. Simultaneous high-pitched calls from others below may form a wall of concentrated sound within the bubbles, packing the bait ball tighter. At the surface, the bubbles and noise enclose the writhing fish. The whales lunge up together, scooping in hundreds of kilos of food in their accordion-like mouths. Is this cooperation instinctive or something the whales learn? The scientists are determined to unpick the puzzle. For Megan Jones, watching the behaviour of females is the best way to observe the interaction of instinct and learning. She's helped by the fact that calves spend their first year only with their mothers, learning the secrets of survival. The focus of my research is really looking at female behaviour during the breeding season in humpback whales. Who did the spy hop, could you tell? Just a nose up, yeah. He did. whales. Basically what we know at this point is we have females that are coming here ostensibly at least for two different reasons. One is to mate and one is to give birth. If you think about it those are two very different objectives that may lead to very different kinds of behaviors that they're doing here and that that's the lens that I'm approaching, how we look at female behaviours. How do females interact with males and does that differ depending upon their reproductive state? The activity descriptions, timings and GPS locations that Megan records are vital data with which she hopes to lay bare the whales' secrets. It's thought that both sexes reach sexual maturity at four to six years old, though the males may not breed till they're much older. It's nearly eleven and a half months before a calf is born. Perfect timing for the return to the breeding grounds. A nursing mother produces around 200 litres of milk per day. As a result, she'll lose up to 10 tons in body weight during the season. She nurtures her calf for a year, leaning it to the feeding grounds and back only once. After that, it must become independent. Cows do seem to be a little bit more playful, at times a little bit more curious. I think they're just kind of, you know, just like a small kid. They're just kind of exploring their environment and learning where their boundaries are and learning what is, you know, what is okay and what's not okay. What you oftentimes see in the water with a mother calf is the mother's oftentimes her head's pointed up to the surface. And because the calf has to come up more often to breathe than the mother does, you know, my guess is she's always keeping a pretty close eye on her calf. And generally speaking, not letting them get too far away. There seems to be a lot of bonding that goes on. It's not uncommon to see the calf rolling on top of the mom's head, to see the calf, you know, resting underneath the mom's chin or belly. The researchers easily spot calves on the surface, but find it frustratingly difficult to draw conclusions about relationships between their mothers and adult males. I don't think we even know whether or not females choose mates within this particular social system. I think that's something we've always presumed is occurring here. But we don't know enough about female behaviour in general to even be able to answer that question at this point. At the University of Queensland in Australia, other scientists are also carrying out humpback research. Heading up part of the project is Dr Michael Node. I've been studying the whales that migrate along the east coast of Australia now for about 12 years. There's about 10,000 whales in that population, and one of the really nice things about them is that they travel really close to the coast during migration. It effectively makes it a bit like a humpback superhighway just off the coast. The main studies that we're doing on the whales involve us listening to the whales and also tracking them, trying to learn as much as we can about the whales as they move through that area. We use a series of hydrophones that we moor offshore which means that we can effectively track singing whales along while they're singing underwater. At the same time we have teams of visual observers up on top of a hill using surveyor's instruments to track all the whales that they can see. So you've got this lovely picture of the way the whales are moving relative to the singers and the way the singers themselves are moving through the area. The song is an amazing acoustic display. It's really complex, it's got these structural rules that govern how it's put together and because it's so complex and so well structured it must be there for a very good reason. What we do know is that only males sing and they only sing during the breeding season so we think it's a mating call of some sort. The main question remains, though, as to whether it's primarily aimed at females as sort of a come-hither type signal to attract females, or whether it's aimed at other males in sort of an aggressive stay away from me. You know, I'm big and I'm singing and I control these bits of water. In Hawaii, Jim's been recording the songs for 30 years. Can his analysis provide the answer to whether the males are attracting females or warning off potential rivals? rivals a male humpback may sing for hours or even days until another whale becomes interested and approaches
